author: @blechnik

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: A whale of a tail, but very interesting non-the-less.

author: @vylbird8014
text:
I see a problem here: The toroidal blades have the advantage of their shape because they prevent vortex formation at the blade tip - a very undesirable thing which makes noise and drag. But you are using a ducted fan - and the duct also works by preventing vortex formation (by physically obstructing the vortex with a the duct). So you're nullifying the advantages of the toroidal blade design: You just have no need for it when you're using it in a duct.

Ducts have their own drawbacks - they add weight, and require precise manufacture to get the blade tips almost-touching the duct. Weight is a big factor for drones, so there's an advantage to toroidal blades instead of ducts for drone use.

author: @plumpdolphin
text: Assuming the results are accurate, I would love to see this sort of thing implemented on a drone! Higher inertia for the blades means that it would likely perform a little worse in applications requiring high maneuverability, but for near-constant rotor speeds like a locked off time lapse shot or sweeping camera shots, a 10% reduction in current draw is nothing to shake a stick at! And that 10% is even at a higher air velocity suggesting it might be closer to 11% - 12% when you lower the duty cycle. Very cool find!

author: @pavanbiliyar
text:
The original Toroidal propeller has a different configuration that you have.  The way you have it, the trailing blade is effectively above the leading blade.  Flip it.

Toroidal propeller configuration is the rotating analogy of "joined wing" designs of airplanes that go back to the 80s.

The idea of the configuration is a leading swept back wing and a trailing swept forward wing are connected at the tips.  Doing this gives you the lift of both wings but the induced flow drag of one wing.

This is because the induced flow as a result of the difference in air pressure over the airfoil causes the tip swirl effect, as if the higher pressure underneath wants to equalize with the lower pressure of the top, but the wing is moving.

Well, in a swept forward wing, the flow moves in the other direction. Inwards along the wing towards the body, there is no tip swirl, no losses due to induced flow affecting drag. 

If we combine both wings, now you see the advantage.  

But as a propeller, if an unducted fan blade tips were connected, thats not a toroidal propeller.  The geometry of the forward swept blade has to suck in the induced flow leaving the tip of the swept back blade.

The way most people 3D print their Toroidal fans, the leading blade is under the trailing blade and their induced flow paths are not connected.  In fact, there's a tip swirl in these designs, defeating the purpose of the propeller.

author: @ewjorgy
text: The slower startup speed may just be a result of higher inertia of the blades with the additional mass of plastic. Those tiny motors don't have a lot of torque.  You should try printing a 3rd setup using a ~1mm high square ridge just a bit back from the leading edge of the blades that would act as a turbulator and see how that works.  When I used to hang glide we would take a line of pinstriping tape and put it across our helmets about 45 degress from vertical from ear to ear.  There was a significant reduction of drag as a result. Turbulators work very well for controlling boundary layer separation in low Renyolds number applications which is what I expect you are seeing with the tubercles...

author: @totherarf
text:
I realise that most of your "stuff" is Proof of Concept and as such does not need refinement to show its purpose.
This may be a tad different though. Tubercles on a blade will alter its characteristics ..... which is what you want after all ..... but you only compare one snapshot. One voltage input giving one output compared to another output. 

But having a variance of voltage inputs would give different speeds and cause you to be able to calculate a power curve of input to output. This would show how to get the max out of the blade design and hence the way different alterations affect those outputs. It would allow you to find the least power to put in to get the most out. This need not be too onerous. Putting 6 or so different speeds in would give a reasonable idea of what is happening. 

Take the example of Drone Blades. They are designed to get the most lift and require high rates of rotation to get that. Now compare it to your desk fan. You use a lower speed and larger blades to get a similar effect if not volume of air moved! Different strokes, so to speak.

Again I realise that you are not honing ideas down too much other than expressing how different ideas can work. This may indeed be work for others to do (and that may be your expectation).
I thank you for putting these ideas up there for others to riff of ..... or to just think about!
